narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like their early and later school grades, LSAT scores, undergraduate GPAs, family income levels, and whether they plan full-time studies—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it calculated a 98% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is why your application was denied; the school uses this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed right away. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to most students' middle rankings, signaling weaker later academic performance to the model. Your LSAT score of 25 was also a strong influence in the same direction, well below the typical 36.7 average that helps others stand out. Even your Year 1 decile of 1 contributed notably toward this outcome, as it was lower than the average student's position. Less impactful but still noted were your middle family income bracket and your undergraduate GPA of 1.8, which is below the 3.21 norm—though the GPA actually nudged slightly toward success. Planning full-time studies helped a little too, aligning with the vast majority of students who pursue that path. Overall, your profile stands out as lower than typical admits in these key academic markers, which outweighed the positives in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've overcome as a Black woman in education—it's just how this tool weighed the data. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later grades if possible, or seeking programs that value holistic reviews beyond these metrics. You're capable of great things; use this feedback to build a stronger path ahead. Keep pushing—you've got this.
